subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let,

L=limn→∞212-213212-215....212-212n+1

Now,

 212-213<1

212-215<1

--------------------

212-212n+1<1   ∀n∈N

And 212-213n<212-213212-215....212-212n+1<212-212n+1n

⇒limn→∞212-213n<limn→∞212-213212-215....212-212n+1<limn→∞212-212n+1n

⇒limn→∞212-213n<L<limn→∞212-212n+1n

And limn→∞212-213n=0  & limn→∞212-212n+1n=0

as  212-213<1 & 212-212n+1<1 

Hence,  limn→∞212-213212-215....212-212n+1=0

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let [·] denote the greatest integer function. We have $f(x) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n^3} \sum_{k=1}^n [\frac{k^2}{3^x}]$.
For large $n$: $\sum_{k=1}^n [\frac{k^2}{3^x}] \approx \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{k^2}{3^x} = \frac{1}{3^x} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} \approx \frac{n^3}{3 \cdot 3^x}$
Therefore: $f(x) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n^3} \cdot \frac{n^3}{3 \cdot 3^x} = \frac{1}{3^{x+1}}$
Computing the infinite series:
$\sum_{j=1}^\infty f(j) = \sum_{j=1}^\infty \frac{1}{3^{j+1}} = \frac{1}{3^2} + \frac{1}{3^3} + \frac{1}{3^4} + \cdots$
$= \frac{1}{9} \cdot (1 + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{9} + \cdots) = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 1/3} = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{2} = \frac{1}{6}$
Therefore: $12 \sum_{j=1}^\infty f(j) = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{6} = 2$

solution:
$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{\tan \left(x / 2^{r+1}\right)+\tan ^3\left(x / 2^{r+1}\right)}{1-\tan ^2\left(x / 2^{r+1}\right)}\right) \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\tan \left(\frac{x}{2^{r+1}}\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{x}{2^r}\right)} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sin \left(x / 2^{r+1}\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{x}{2^{r+1}}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x}{2^r}\right)} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sin \left(\frac{x}{2^r}-\frac{x}{2^{r+1}}\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{x}{2^{r+1}}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x}{2^r}\right)}\end{aligned}$
$=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \tan \left(\frac{x}{2^r}\right)-\tan \left(\frac{x}{2^{r+1}}\right)$
From condition given question
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{r=0}^n\left[\tan \left(\frac{x}{2^r}\right)-\tan \left(\frac{x}{2^{r+1}}\right)\right]=\tan x \\
& \therefore \lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^x-e^{\tan x}}{x-\tan x}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} e^{\tan x}\left(\frac{e^{x-\tan x}-1}{x-\tan x}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} e^{\tan x} \lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{x-\tan x}-1}{x-\tan x}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow 1.1\left(\because \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{x-1}}{x}=1\right) \\
& \quad=1
\end{aligned}$

⇒limx→12−x312−x12=144

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \operatorname{cosec}\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}-\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right) \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\cos \operatorname{ec} x\left(\cos ^2 x+3 \cos x-\sin x-4\right)}{\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\sin x} \frac{\left(\cos ^2 x+3 \cos x-4\right)-\sin x}{\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(\cos ^x+4\right)(\cos x-1)-\sin x}{\sin x\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{-2 \sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}(\cos x+4)-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{-\left(\sin ^x(\cos x+4)+\cos \frac{x}{2}\right)}{\cos \frac{x}{2}\left(\sqrt{2 \cos ^2 x+3 \cos x}+\sqrt{\cos ^2 x+\sin x+4}\right)} \\ & -\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{5}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given limit is $\lim\limits_{x \to 2} \dfrac{\tan(x-2)}{x-2} \cdot \dfrac{rx^2 + (p-2)x - 2p}{x-2} = 5$

Since $\lim\limits_{x \to 2} \dfrac{\tan(x-2)}{x-2} = 1$, we have:

$\lim\limits_{x \to 2} \dfrac{rx^2 + (p-2)x - 2p}{x-2} = 5$

For the limit to exist, the numerator must be zero at $x = 2$:

$r(2)^2 + (p-2)(2) - 2p = 0$

$4r + 2p - 4 - 2p = 0 \Rightarrow 4r = 4 \Rightarrow r = 1$

Substituting $r = 1$ into the limit expression:

$\lim\limits_{x \to 2} \dfrac{x^2 + (p-2)x - 2p}{x-2} = 5$

$\lim\limits_{x \to 2} \dfrac{(x-2)(x+p)}{x-2} = 5$

$\lim\limits_{x \to 2} (x+p) = 5 \Rightarrow 2 + p = 5 \Rightarrow p = 3$

The quadratic equation is $rx^2 - px + q = 0$, which becomes $x^2 - 3x + q = 0$.

For both roots of $f(x) = x^2 - 3x + q = 0$ to lie in the interval $(0, 2)$, the following conditions must hold:

1) Discriminant $D \ge 0 \Rightarrow (-3)^2 - 4(1)(q) \ge 0 \Rightarrow 9 - 4q \ge 0 \Rightarrow q \le \dfrac{9}{4}$

2) $f(0) > 0 \Rightarrow q > 0$

3) $f(2) > 0 \Rightarrow 2^2 - 3(2) + q > 0 \Rightarrow q - 2 > 0 \Rightarrow q > 2$

4) The $x$-coordinate of the vertex must lie in $(0, 2) \Rightarrow 0 < \dfrac{-(-3)}{2(1)} < 2 \Rightarrow 0 < 1.5 < 2$, which is true.

Taking the intersection of all conditions, we get $q \in (2, \dfrac{9}{4}]$.

Comparing this with $(\alpha, \beta]$, we have $\alpha = 2$ and $\beta = \dfrac{9}{4}$.

Therefore, $4(\alpha + \beta) = 4(2 + \dfrac{9}{4}) = 8 + 9 = 17$.

Answer: $17$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
We know that r≤r<r+1

2r≤2r<2r+1

3r≤3r<3r+1

⋮  ⋮   ⋮

nr≤nr<nr+1

On adding all the above inequalities, we get

r+2r+…+nr≤r+2r+...+nr<r+2r+...nr+n

Using 1+2+3+...+n=nn+12, n∈N,

nn+12·r≤r+2r+...+nr<nn+12·r+n

⇒nn+12·rn2≤r+2r+...+nrn2<nn+12·r+nn2

Now, limn→∞n(n+1)·r2·n2=limn→∞n21+1n·r2·n2=r2

and limn→∞nn+12·r+nn2=limn→∞n21+1n·r+1n2·n2=r2

So, by Sandwich Theorem, we can conclude that

limn→∞r+2r+...+nrn2=r2.

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\left(r^2-r\right)(n-r)}{\sum_{r=1}^n r^3-\sum_{r=1}^n r^2} \\ & \lim _{\mathrm{n} \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}-1}\left(-\mathrm{r}^3+\mathrm{r}^2(\mathrm{n}+1)-\mathrm{nr}\right)}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}\right)^2-\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6}} \\ & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\left(\frac{((n-1) n)}{2}\right)^2+\frac{(n+1)(n-1) n(2 n-1)}{6}-\frac{n^2(n-1)}{2}}{\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}-\frac{2 n+1}{3}\right)} \\ & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\left(\frac{-n(n-1)}{2}+\frac{(n+1)(2 n-1)}{3}-n\right)}{\frac{n(n+1)}{2} \frac{3 n^2+3 n-4 n-2}{6}} \\ & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{(n-1)\left(-3 n^2+3 n+2\left(2 n^2+n-1\right)-6\right)}{(n+1)\left(3 n^2-n-2\right)} \\ & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{(n-1)\left(n^2+5 n-8\right)}{(n+1)\left(3 n^2-n-2\right)}=\frac{1}{3} \\ & \end{aligned}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{e}^{\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\tan x-\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}^3}\right)}$
$=\mathrm{e}^{\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(x+\frac{x^3}{3}+\frac{2 x^5}{15}+\ldots . .-x\right)}{x^3}}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
RHL is, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{\tan \left(\pi \sin ^{2} x\right)+(x-0)^{2}}{x^{2}}$

$=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}\left(\frac{\tan \left(\pi \sin ^{2} x\right.}{x^{2}}+1\right)=1+\pi$

And LHL is, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{\tan \left(\pi \sin ^{2} x\right)+(-x+\sin x)^{2}}{x^{2}}$

$=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{\tan \left(\pi \sin ^{2} x\right)+x^{2}+\sin ^{2} x-2 x \sin x}{x^{2}}$

$=\pi+1+1-2=\pi$

Since, LHL $\neq$ RHL Hence, limit does not exist.

$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{x}-\frac{2}{e^{2 x}-1}$
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{2 x}-1-2 x}{x\left(e^{2 x}-1\right)}$
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 e^{2 x}-2}{\left(e^{2 x}-1\right)+2 x e^{2 x}}$
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{4 e^{2 x}}{4 e^{2 x}+4 x e^{2 x}}=1$.
